skill.name: tzst
skill.description: Use when the user needs to create, extract, flatten, list, test, install, script, or troubleshoot `tzst` CLI workflows for `.tzst` or `.tar.zst` archives, including compression levels, streaming mode, extraction filters, conflict resolution, JSON output, or standalone binary setup, even if they describe the archive task without naming `tzst`.
skill.summary: CLI skill for Zstandard compression tool (tzst) - create, extract, list, test .tzst archives with safe defaults
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: All 125 static findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate CLI documentation guide for the Zstandard compression tool (tzst). Backticks in markdown are code formatting for CLI examples, not shell execution. URLs point to the project's own releases page. Zstandard is a modern compression algorithm, not a weak cryptographic algorithm.

risk_factor_evidence.evaluation: FALSE_POSITIVE - Backticks in markdown are code formatting for CLI examples like `tzst --version`, not Ruby/shell execution
risk_factor_evidence.confidence_reasoning: The static scanner detects backtick syntax in markdown as potential shell execution, but these are documented CLI commands in code blocks

risk_factor_evidence.evaluation: FALSE_POSITIVE - URLs point to the project's own GitHub releases page (github.com/xixu-me/tzst) for installation instructions
risk_factor_evidence.confidence_reasoning: Self-referential URLs for legitimate installation guidance - not external data exfiltration

content.value_statement: This skill helps Claude users work with Zstandard compressed archives (.tzst, .tar.zst) by providing a comprehensive CLI reference with safe defaults, security filters, and automation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Create .tzst and .tar.zst compressed archives with configurable compression levels
Extract archives while preserving directory structure or flattening output
List archive contents and test archive integrity
Provide JSON output for scripting and automation
Guide safe extraction with security filters (data, tar, fully_trusted)
Help configure conflict resolution for automated extraction
limitations: CLI-only skill - does not cover Python API usage (from tzst import ...)
Requires tzst to be installed on the system
Does not handle other archive formats like zip, tar.gz, or 7z

use_cases.title: Backup automation scripts
use_cases.description: DevOps engineers creating automated backup scripts that compress directories and verify archive integrity

use_cases.title: Archive extraction assistance
use_cases.description: Users who need to extract .tzst archives from downloads or backups with safe defaults

use_cases.title: Large file compression
use_cases.description: Users working with large datasets or log files that need efficient compression

prompt_templates.title: Create a compressed archive
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tzst archive called backup.tzst containing the documents/ folder. Use default compression level.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to compress files into a .tzst archive

prompt_templates.title: Extract an archive
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the backup.tzst archive to a restore/ directory, preserving the original directory structure. Use the safest filter.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to extract a .tzst archive safely

prompt_templates.title: List archive contents
prompt_templates.prompt: List all files in the backup.tzst archive with details (verbose mode).
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to see what is inside an archive before extracting

prompt_templates.title: Automated extraction
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract archive.tzst to output/ with JSON output and auto-rename all conflicts. Do not prompt for user input.

output_examples.input: Create a tzst archive with photos from my vacation
output_examples.output: I'll create a compressed archive for your photos. First, let me check if tzst is installed...

output_examples.input: Extract this backup.tzst file to a restore folder
output_examples.output: I'll extract the archive using safe defaults that preserve your directory structure...

output_examples.input: List what files are inside data.tzst
output_examples.output: Let me check the contents of the archive for you...

best_practices: Use the 'x' (extract) command instead of 'e' (extract-flat) unless flattening is explicitly requested
Prefer --filter data as the default extraction mode for untrusted archives
Use --json --no-banner for scripting and automation to avoid parsing issues
Set explicit --conflict-resolution values for automated extraction to avoid blocking on prompts
anti_patterns: Using 'e' (extract-flat) when the user expects directory structure to be preserved
Recommending 'fully_trusted' filter for archives from unknown sources
Guessing flags from tar habits instead of checking the tzst reference

faq.question: What is tzst?
faq.answer: tzst is a CLI tool for Zstandard compression. It creates and extracts .tzst and .tar.zst compressed archives with modern compression ratios and fast performance.

faq.question: How do I install tzst?
faq.answer: Install via 'uv tool install tzst' or 'pip install tzst'. For standalone use, download a binary from github.com/xixu-me/tzst/releases/latest.

faq.question: What is the difference between 'x' and 'e' commands?
faq.answer: 'x' (extract) preserves directory structure. 'e' (extract-flat) flattens all files into one directory. Prefer 'x' unless flattening is explicitly requested.

faq.question: Which extraction filter is safest?
faq.answer: The 'data' filter is the safest default. Use 'tar' for standard compatibility, and 'fully_trusted' only when you completely trust the archive source.

faq.question: Can I use tzst in scripts?
faq.answer: Yes, use '--json --no-banner' for machine-readable output. Set explicit '--conflict-resolution' values (like replace_all) to avoid interactive prompts.

faq.question: What compression level should I use?
faq.answer: Level 3 is the default and provides a good balance. Use higher levels (up to 10) for better compression at the cost of slower speed.
